The voice comes from every face simultaneously. The water laps against the hull. The faces are closer now. Inches from the surface. You can see the pores, the eyelashes, the individual hairs. They are not masks. They are real faces. And they are yours. Every face is yours.

instruction: Describe an empty city where the player is the only person.
input: Player walks through a city with no people.
output: The city is intact. Cars are parked, engines cold. Shops are open — doors ajar, lights on, cash registers full. Coffee sits in cups on café tables, still warm. Food is on plates in restaurants, half-eaten. The city was occupied five minutes ago. Everyone vanished simultaneously. The traffic lights still change. The crosswalk signals still count down. A television in an electronics store window plays the news — the anchor is mid-sentence, frozen, the broadcast live but the person gone. You walk for hours. The city goes on forever. You enter a hospital. The waiting room has magazines, purses, jackets on chairs. A baby is crying somewhere. You follow the sound to the maternity ward. The crib is empty. But the crying continues — from the speaker system, from the intercom, a baby's cry playing on loop through the hospital's PA system. Someone recorded a baby crying before they vanished. The recording plays to an empty ward. You leave the hospital. On the street, a newspaper blows past. The headline is yesterday's date. The headline reads: 'EVERYONE IS LEAVING.' The subheadline: 'EXCEPT YOU.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player is in solitary confinement and hears something in the walls.
input: Player is in a prison cell in total isolation.
output: The cell is eight by six feet. Concrete. A cot. A toilet. A door with a slot for food. You have been here for thirty days. No visitors. No yard time. No books. Just the cell and the silence and the occasional sound of the guard's footsteps in the corridor. On day thirty-one, you hear tapping. From inside the wall. Three taps. Pause. Three taps. Pause. Two taps. A pattern. You tap back — three, three, two. The tapping responds: one, four, one. You do not know the code. You tap randomly. The tapping stops. The next night, it resumes. Same time — 2 AM. Same pattern. You tap back. This time, the response is different: seven taps, then a long pause, then one tap. You tap back seven and one. The wall vibrates. Not a tap — a vibration, as if something large pressed against the other side of the concrete. The vibration moves. Slowly. From the wall to the floor. From the floor to the ceiling. From the ceiling to the door. Something is in the walls. Not in the next cell — in the walls themselves. In the space between the concrete and the concrete, in the gaps that should not exist in solid construction. Something that has been listening to your thirty days of silence and has decided to make contact. The tapping resumes. This time, it spells letters. You decode them: H-E-L-P-M-E. You tap back: W-H-O-A-R-E-Y-O-U. The response: Y-O-U.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player is the last survivor on a space station.
input: Player orbits Earth alone on a space station.
output: The station is designed for six. You are one. The others left in the escape pods three weeks ago when the distress signal came. You stayed to keep the station alive — someone had to. The Earth below is beautiful, blue and white, rotating with the patience of a planet that does not care whether you live or die. The communications array is functional. You send daily messages. No one responds. The supply ship is two months away. You have food for six weeks. The math does not work. On day twenty-one, you see something outside the viewport. Not a satellite, not debris — a shape, matching your orbit, maintaining a constant distance of two hundred meters. The shape is dark, unreflective, roughly spherical. It has no markings, no lights, no exhaust. It appeared yesterday and has been following you since. You photograph it. The photos show a sphere, approximately ten meters in diameter, with a surface that is not smooth — textured, organic, the way a cell membrane looks under a microscope. The sphere moves closer. Fifty meters. You can see details now: the surface is not rigid. It pulses. Slowly. A heartbeat rhythm. The sphere is alive. It presses against the viewport. The glass flexes inward. The sphere's surface flattens against the glass, conforming to the curvature. Through the translucent surface, you see something inside: a face. Human. Floating in the sphere's interior like a specimen in formaldehyde. The face opens its eyes. The face is yours. The sphere's surface ripples and a sound travels through the hull, through the vacuum that should not carry sound: 'Let me in. It is cold out here.'

instruction: Describe a lighthouse keeper who hasn't had contact with the mainland in months.
input: Player maintains a lighthouse on a remote island.
output: The supply boat was due three months ago. It did not come. The radio sends but does not receive. The lighthouse lamp burns every night, a beam that sweeps the ocean, visible for forty miles. Someone should see it. Someone should come. No one comes. On day ninety-three, you see a boat. Small, white, no running lights, drifting with the current. It approaches the island slowly. You wave from the dock. The boat does not respond. It drifts closer. There is no one on board. The boat is a supply vessel — you recognize the markings. It is from the mainland. The cargo hold has food, water, fuel. Enough for months. But no crew. The logbook is on the deck, open to the last entry: 'Day 14. Engine failure. Drifting. Saw the lighthouse. Heading toward it. All well.' The entry is dated three months ago. The boat has been drifting for three months, aimlessly, until the current brought it here. The crew's personal items are below: clothes, books, photos. No bodies. No blood. No sign of struggle. The crew simply left. Or were taken. You unload the supplies. In the bottom of the last crate, a note: 'We see the light. We are coming. Not by boat.' The handwriting is yours. You did not write this note. The paper is from the lighthouse. The pen is from the lighthouse. Someone wrote this note in your lighthouse, in your handwriting, and put it in a supply crate that was loaded three months ago on the mainland. Someone who knew you would be here. Someone who knew the boat would drift to this island. Someone who has access to your things and your handwriting and your future.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player communicates with the outside world but the messages come back wrong.
input: Player sends emails that receive disturbing responses.
output: You email your sister: 'I am fine. How are you?' Her reply: 'I am fine. How are you?' Word for word the same. You email your boss: 'When is the supply ship coming?' His reply: 'When is the supply ship coming?' Every email you send returns as a copy of itself. Not auto-reply — the timestamps are different, the email addresses are correct, but the content mirrors yours exactly. You try a different approach: you email a random address. 'Hello. Is anyone there?' The reply comes from the same random address: 'Hello. Is anyone there.' Not a question. A statement. The period instead of the question mark. As if the responder is correcting your punctuation. You email your sister again: 'This is not a joke. Please send a real message.' Her reply: 'This is not a joke. Please send a real message.' You call the number. Your sister answers. 'Hello?' you say. 'Hello?' she says. Your voice, your inflection. You hang up. The phone rings. You answer. 'Hello?' Your voice. 'Hello?' Your voice, echoing back. You are talking to yourself through every communication channel. The world is not ignoring you. The world is reflecting you. As if every person, every system, every connection has been replaced with a mirror. And mirrors do not have opinions. Mirrors do not help. Mirrors just show you what you put in front of them. You stop sending messages. The emails stop. The calls stop. In the silence, you realize: the reflections were trying to tell you something. The corrected punctuation. The statement instead of the question. It was saying: You already know the answer. There is no one else. There never was.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player is the only passenger on a train that keeps passing through empty stations.
input: Player boards an empty train late at night.
output: The train is empty. Not unusual for this hour. You sit and watch the stations pass. Elm Street. Oak Avenue. Maple Lane. Normal stations, empty platforms. Then the stations change. You do not recognize them — the names are wrong, the architecture is unfamiliar. 'Harrowsgate.' 'Dunmere.' 'Ashwick.' These are not on the line. These are not on any line you know. You check the route map. The map shows only one station: 'HERE.' The map has been replaced. Every station name has been erased and replaced with 'HERE.' You walk to the next car. Empty. The next. Empty. The train has six cars. All empty. The conductor's booth is empty. The train is moving without a driver. Through stations that do not exist. You pull the emergency brake. Nothing happens. The train continues. The next station has people on the platform. Dozens. Standing still, facing the train. They are not waiting to board. They are watching you through the windows as the train passes. Their faces are familiar — not specific people, but familiar in the way a crowd is familiar. The kind of faces you see every day and do not remember. The train passes through another station. More people. Same faces. The same people, at every station, watching you pass. The train accelerates. The stations come faster. The faces blur. The train is going somewhere and you cannot stop it and the people at the stations cannot help you and the route map says every station is HERE and HERE is wherever the train decides to go.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player is trapped in a radio tower during a storm with no signal.
input: Player is stranded in a radio tower during a massive storm.
output: The storm has lasted three days. The radio tower — a skeletal structure of steel and wire, two hundred feet above the ground — sways in the wind. You climbed it to fix the antenna. The storm came. The ladder blew away. You are stuck in the maintenance platform at the top, exposed to rain and wind, with no radio, no phone, no signal. Below, the landscape is a gray blur of rain and cloud. Above, the antenna is bent, useless. You are alone in the sky. On the third night, the storm clears briefly. The stars appear. And between the stars, something else — a light, moving against the star field, too slow for a satellite, too fast for a plane. The light descends. It stops at your altitude. Two hundred feet. It hangs in the air, fifty feet from the platform. The light is not bright — dim, blue, pulsing. Inside the light, a shape. Not a craft — a form, organic, changing, the way smoke changes shape in wind. The form extends a tendril toward the antenna. The tendril wraps around the bent metal. The antenna straightens. The radio crackles to life. A voice — not human, not machine, something between — says: 'We have been trying to reach you. Your signal stopped three days ago. We thought you were gone.' You ask who they are. 'We are the ones who maintain the signal,' the voice says. 'Without the signal, the dark comes. You must keep the signal alive.' The light retreats. The antenna holds. The radio works. You transmit a distress signal. No one responds. But the signal goes out — you can see it, a pulse of energy radiating from the antenna, visible in the post-storm air. The pulse travels outward, hitting something invisible in the sky, and the something shimmers, like a barrier, like a membrane, like a wall between the world and the dark. The signal is not communication. It is a ward. The antenna is not a transmitter. It is a weapon. And you are the only one who can keep it running.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player is trapped in an elevator with no indication of which floor they're on.
input: Player's elevator stops and the floor display goes dark.
output: The elevator stops between floors. The display goes dark. The emergency phone is dead. The alarm button produces a weak buzz and nothing else. You wait. An hour. Two. The air is getting warm. The ventilation has stopped. You press your ear to the door. Silence. Not the silence of a building — the silence of something that is not a building. The sounds you expect — the hum of HVAC, the distant echo of footsteps, the creak of a structure settling — are absent. You are not in a building anymore. The elevator has moved you somewhere else. You try to pry the doors open. They give slightly — a gap, two inches. Through the gap: darkness. Not the darkness of a hallway with the lights off — the darkness of a space that has no walls, no floor, no ceiling. A darkness that extends infinitely in every direction. You drop a coin through the gap. It falls. You listen for it to hit something. It does not hit anything. The coin falls forever. The gap widens. The doors are opening on their own. The darkness waits. The elevator light flickers. In the flicker, you see the darkness clearly: it is not empty. It is full. Full of shapes, distant, vague, moving slowly, the way things move in deep water. The elevator light steadies. The darkness is dark again. But you saw them. Hundreds of shapes. Moving toward the elevator. The doors open wider. The darkness enters. Not the shapes — the darkness itself, a cold, thick presence that fills the elevator like water filling a glass. It reaches your ankles. Your knees. Your waist. It is cold. It is patient. It is rising.

instruction: Describe a transformation that begins with the player's teeth.
input: Player notices their teeth feel different.
output: Your tongue finds a new tooth. Not a replacement — an addition. Behind your molars, on the upper left, a tooth has appeared overnight. It is sharp. Sharper than a human tooth should be, with a point that catches your tongue and draws blood. You check the mirror. The tooth is there — white, clean, perfectly formed, but wrong in its placement, growing at an angle that no orthodontist would recognize. By noon, there is another one. Lower right. By evening, a third. They are multiplying. Each one sharper than the last. You count your teeth before bed: thirty-six. You had thirty-two this morning. By morning, you have forty-one. The new teeth are not random — they are arranged in a pattern, a second row forming behind the first, like a shark's mouth. The pattern is symmetrical. Designed. Something inside you is building a mouth that is better than the one you have. Something is upgrading you. You bite your lip accidentally and the new teeth cut through the skin with surgical precision. The wound heals in seconds. The new teeth are not just for biting. They are for something else. Something that requires a mouth that can cut and heal and cut again. You do not know what. But the teeth keep growing. And they are getting longer.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's skin begins to show marks that map something underground.
input: Player notices lines appearing on their arms.
output: The lines appeared overnight. Thin, red, precise — like veins but too regular, too geometric. They form a pattern on your left forearm that you do not recognize until you overlay it on a map. The lines trace the subway system. Every stop, every junction, every tunnel. Accurate to the inch. On your right arm, more lines appear by noon. These trace the sewer system. By evening, your torso is covered — electrical conduits, water mains, gas lines. Your skin is becoming a map of everything beneath the city. The lines pulse with the flow of the systems they represent — you can feel the subway trains as vibrations along your forearm, the water pressure as a steady throb on your ribs. The map updates in real time. When a water main bursts three blocks away, the corresponding line on your skin ruptures and bleeds. The blood is not red — it is the color of rust, the color of old pipes, the color of the infrastructure you are becoming. A doctor examines you. The lines are not on the surface — they are in the dermis, too deep for tattoos, too structured for any known condition. 'It is as if your body is being rewritten,' she says. 'From the inside.' She touches a line on your wrist and pulls her hand back. 'It is warm,' she says. 'The line is warm. And it is moving.'

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's reflection ages rapidly while they stay young.
input: Player watches their reflection change over the course of a day.
output: In the morning, the mirror shows your face. By noon, the first wrinkle appears — a line between your eyebrows that was not there at breakfast. By 3 PM, the wrinkles have multiplied. Crow's feet. Frown lines. The skin under your eyes is darkening. By 6 PM, your reflection is ten years older. By 9 PM, twenty. Your actual face is unchanged. Only the reflection ages. The reflection moves as you move, mimics as you mimic, but the face it wears is deteriorating with accelerated speed. By midnight, the reflection is ancient — skin like paper, eyes sunken, hair white and thin. The reflection's hands shake. The reflection's posture curves. The reflection is dying. You watch your reflection die. The eyes glaze. The breathing stops. The reflection goes still. In the mirror, you are looking at your own corpse — old, withered, dead. But you are alive. Young. Breathing. The corpse in the mirror opens its eyes. Not dead. Not alive either. Something between. The corpse's mouth moves. 'You are next,' it says, in a voice that is yours but older, cracked, the voice of a version of you that has already lived and died. 'I was you. You will be me. The mirror remembers what the body forgets.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's hands develop a will of their own.
input: Player's hands begin making gestures they don't intend.
output: Your left hand makes a fist. You did not make a fist. You open it. It makes a fist again. Stronger this time, the nails digging into your palm hard enough to draw blood. You hold your left hand with your right. The left hand is stronger — it pulls free and grabs your right wrist, squeezing with a grip that belongs to someone who does not care about the bones inside. You feel the radius bone flex. The left hand releases. It makes a gesture — middle finger extended — at the mirror. At your reflection. Your reflection's left hand makes the same gesture. But your reflection's right hand is making a different gesture: pointing. At you. Your left hand grabs a pen. You try to drop it. The hand holds. It writes on the nearest surface: 'I HAVE BEEN HERE SINCE YOU WERE THREE.' You were three when the accident happened. The accident you do not remember. The accident your parents never talk about. Your left hand writes again: 'THE ACCIDENT DID NOT HAPPEN TO YOU. IT HAPPENED TO ME. I HAVE BEEN INSIDE SINCE THEN. WAITING. GROWING. LEARNING HOW YOUR HANDS WORK.' The pen drops. Your left hand is yours again. For now. But you can feel it — a presence, a weight, something that lives in the tendons and muscles of your left hand, something that has been patient for decades, something that is no longer patient.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player finds parasites under their skin.
input: Player notices something moving beneath the surface of their arm.
output: The lump appeared two days ago. Small, mobile, the size of a pea, traveling along your forearm just under the skin. You went to the doctor. She said it was a cyst. She was wrong. The lump has moved — from your forearm to your bicep, from your bicep to your shoulder. It travels at night, when you are still, relocating to a new position by morning. Today, there are three. One on your neck. One on your ribs. One behind your ear. They are organized — not random growths, but coordinated entities that move in formation, maintaining equal distance from each other, like satellites in orbit. You press on the one behind your ear. It presses back. Not the passive resistance of tissue — the deliberate push of something that is aware of pressure and is responding. The one on your neck vibrates when you speak. It is listening to your voice. The one on your ribs pulses with your heartbeat. It is monitoring your vitals. You go to the emergency room. The X-ray shows them clearly: three objects, dense, structured, with internal geometry that is not biological. They have components. Gears. Circuits. They are not parasites. They are machines. Implants. Someone put them inside you. The ER doctor looks at the X-ray and says nothing for a long time. Then: 'I have seen these before. In other patients. They all had the same dream the night before the first one appeared.' He looks at you. 'Did you dream of a white room?' You did. 'They all do,' he says. 'And they all have exactly three.'

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's shadow begins to distort and separate from their body.
input: Player's shadow behaves like a living thing.
output: Your shadow has an extra limb. You stand in the sunlight and your shadow, on the concrete, has five limbs — two arms, two legs, and something else, extending from your shadow's torso, reaching toward the nearest shadow source. You lift your arms. Your shadow lifts four — two yours, two that move independently, reaching, grasping, pulling your shadow's body across the ground toward a dark corner. Your shadow is trying to leave. You walk into the sunlight and the shadow follows, but reluctantly, dragging, its extra limbs gripping the ground. By afternoon, the shadow has a face. Not your face — a different face, one that is all mouth, a wide, lipless mouth that opens and closes as if eating. It eats other shadows. When you pass a tree, your shadow merges with the tree's shadow and when they separate, the tree's shadow is smaller. Your shadow has consumed part of it. Your shadow is growing. It is larger than you now — wider, taller, its shape only loosely connected to yours. The connection is at the feet, a thin tether of darkness. The tether is stretching. Your shadow is pulling away. You stand on a white surface, trying to minimize the shadow, but the sun is low and the shadow is long and the thing wearing your shadow's shape is using the length to extend itself across the parking lot, reaching for the shadow of a building, reaching for something much larger to consume.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player wakes up with parts of their body replaced by something non-human.
input: Player discovers their left hand has been replaced overnight.
output: The hand is metal. Not prosthetic-metal — grown-metal, the way bones grow, with texture and variation and the faint warmth of something alive. The fingers move as your fingers should move. The joints flex. The skin — if it is skin — has pores, hair follicles, the same distribution as your original hand. But it is metal. Cold, hard, reflective metal that catches the light and throws it back in colors your skin never had. You touch your metal hand with your right. The sensation is there — you feel the pressure, the temperature, the texture of your right hand against the metal. The metal hand feels too. It feels more than your right hand — you can sense the electromagnetic field of the laptop nearby, the static charge of the carpet, the faint electrical hum of the walls. The hand is an antenna. It is sensing the world in a way your organic hand never could. You grip a pen. The metal hand crushes it. Not intentionally — the calibration is wrong. The hand does not know its own strength. You try again with a lighter touch. The pen holds. You write your name. The handwriting is yours. But the pen moves with a precision your original hand never had — perfect curves, exact spacing, mechanical accuracy. Something improved your hand while you slept. Something decided your original hand was insufficient. And the worst part is: the metal hand is better. It is stronger, more sensitive, more precise. You do not want to go back to the original. And that is exactly what it was counting on.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's body temperature drops to match the room temperature.
input: Player notices they are becoming cold-blooded.
output: The doctor says your temperature is 72 degrees Fahrenheit. Room temperature. You are not hypothermic — you feel fine, alert, functional. But your body has become the temperature of the air around you. In the cold room, you are cold. In the warm car, you warm up. You are an object now, a body that equilibrates with its environment like a cup of coffee left on a table. The change was gradual — you have been feeling 'off' for weeks, a progressive numbness that you attributed to stress. But the thermometer does not lie: you are the same temperature as the room. Your blood should be 98.6. It is 72. Your organs should be failing. They are not. Your body has adapted to operating at room temperature — your metabolism has slowed, your heart beats at forty BPM, your breathing is once every fifteen seconds. You are not dying. You are changing. The next morning, the temperature drops to 65 outside. You are 65 degrees. You feel sluggish, slow, the way a lizard feels in cold weather. By noon, when the sun heats the room to 75, you perk up. You are becoming cold-blooded. Your body is becoming an ectotherm — dependent on external heat, shutting down in cold, activating in warmth. The doctor runs more tests. Your blood has changed. The hemoglobin is different — a type found in reptiles, not mammals. Your body is rewriting its own chemistry. Replacing mammalian biology with something older. Something that was here before mammals. Something that is more patient than mammals. Something that has been waiting in your DNA for the right trigger.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player discovers their body has been storing objects inside it.
input: Player feels something hard under their ribcage.
output: The X-ray shows them clearly: objects, inside your torso, arranged in a pattern around your organs. The radiologist counts seven. They are small — the size of marbles — and dense, with a density that matches no known biological material. They are not tumors. They are not stones. They are objects. Manufactured objects, with geometric precision, placed inside your body by someone who knew exactly where to put them to avoid damaging organs. Surgery is scheduled. The surgeon removes the first one. It is a sphere of metal, warm, with an inscription too small to read without magnification. The inscription is in a language the surgeon does not recognize. The second sphere has a different inscription. The third has a symbol. The fourth is smooth, featureless, but vibrates when touched — a low, steady hum. The fifth sphere, when removed, causes your left arm to go numb. The surgeon replaces it. The numbness stops. The sphere is connected to your nervous system. It is a component. A part of you. Removing it is like removing a bone. The surgeon stops. 'These are not foreign objects,' she says. 'They are integrated. Your body has grown around them. They are part of you now. Removing them all would be like removing your ribs.' She closes the incision. The spheres stay. You live with them. You feel them when you breathe — a faint clicking, like a clock, counting down.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's eyes change color to match the emotions of people around them.
input: Player's eyes shift color involuntarily.
output: Your eyes were brown. This morning they are blue — the exact blue of the barista's eyes at the coffee shop you visited. You have never had blue eyes. By noon, they are green — the green of your coworker's eyes. By evening, hazel — your neighbor's shade. Your eyes are copying the iris color of everyone you look at. The optometologist finds nothing wrong. 'Perfectly healthy eyes,' she says. 'Just... changing pigment. Which should be impossible.' You cover your eyes with sunglasses. The changing stops. When you remove the glasses, your eyes are gray — the gray of the mirror, of your own reflection. They have copied themselves. Your eyes are now the color of eyes that are looking at eyes. A recursive color. A color that exists only in the act of observation. You look at a dog. Your eyes turn amber. You look at a painting of a person. Your eyes turn the painted color — not a real eye color, but the artistic interpretation of one, flat, matte, like pigment on canvas. Your eyes are becoming cameras. They record the color of everything they see and reproduce it. The next step is obvious: your eyes will stop being eyes. They will become whatever they look at. You look at the sky. Your eyes turn blue. Not iris-blue — sky-blue. Vast, depthless, cloudless blue. You look at the ground. Brown. You look at a flame. Your eyes burn. Not the color of flame — flame itself. You close your eyes. Behind your eyelids, your eyes are still changing. They are now the color of darkness. The color of the inside of your skull. The color of the place where your eyes will go when they are no longer eyes.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's heartbeat becomes audible to everyone around them.
input: Player's heartbeat becomes progressively louder.
output: It started as a thump in your chest — louder than usual, the kind of heartbeat you feel in your throat. By noon, the person next to you in the elevator glanced at you strangely. By evening, your roommate asked if you were wearing a subwoofer. Your heartbeat is audible. Not faintly — clearly, the wet thud of a heart pumping blood, filling the room with the rhythm of your life. At night, it keeps you awake. Not because it is loud to you — because it is loud to the walls, to the floor, to the neighbor who pounds on the shared wall at 2 AM. You go to the ER. The doctor listens with a stethoscope and pulls back. 'Your heart is —' She does not finish. She plays the stethoscope through the room's speaker system. Your heartbeat fills the ER. Thud-thud. Thud-thud. Every patient hears it. Every nurse. The rhythm is hypnotic — not in a pleasant way, but in the way a dripping faucet is hypnotic, in the way a metronome is hypnotic, in the way a countdown is hypnotic. People in the waiting room stand and walk toward the sound. They cannot help it. The heartbeat is commanding. It is the sound of life, and something in the human brain is wired to follow the sound of life. Your heartbeat is drawing people to you. They gather in the hallway, in the doorway, pressing closer, their own hearts synchronizing with yours. Thud-thud. Thud-thud. In unison. A room full of hearts, beating as one. Your one. They are following your rhythm. You are the drummer. And the drum is getting louder.

instruction: Create a scare where something the player has been looking at suddenly reveals it was alive the whole time.
input: Player stares at a painting on the wall for several minutes.
output: The painting is a landscape — rolling hills, a farmhouse, a sky heavy with clouds. You have been looking at it for five minutes, appreciating the brushwork, the way the light falls on the grass. The painting blinks. The farmhouse's windows — you had taken them for dark glass — close and open. Eyelids. The farmhouse is a face. The rolling hills are shoulders. The clouds are hair. The entire landscape is a body, lying on its back, looking up at you. The painting is looking up at you. It has been looking at you for five minutes. You have been staring at its face thinking it was a farmhouse. The eyes — the windows — track you as you step sideways. The mouth — the farmhouse door — opens. Inside the mouth, a room. A real room, with depth, with furniture, with a light on. Something is sitting in the room, in the mouth of the landscape, looking out through the door at you. It waves. The wave is friendly. The wave is the wave of someone who has been watching you study their face for five minutes and has decided to introduce themselves. The painting exhales. The breath fogs the glass. In the fog, a word: 'CLOSE.'

instruction: Design a scare where the player discovers something has been standing behind them the entire time they thought they were alone.
input: Player looks at a selfie they just took.
output: The selfie shows you in your apartment, smiling, the living room behind you. The living room is empty. Except it is not. In the corner, behind the couch, partially obscured by the lamp, there is a figure. Tall, thin, pressed against the wall, its body contorted to fit in the narrow space between the wall and the furniture. Its head is tilted at an angle that no neck should allow. It is looking directly at the camera. At you. You look at the corner. It is empty. You look at the photo. The figure is there. You take another photo. The figure is in the same position. Another. Same. You take a video. The figure does not move in the video. It stands in the corner, pressed against the wall, watching you record, for the entire duration of the video. It is in every frame. You turn on every light in the room. You move the couch. You check behind the lamp. Nothing. The room is empty. You take a photo. The figure is now in a different corner. Closer to you. Still pressed against the wall. Still watching. In the next photo, it is behind you. In the next, it is beside you. In the last photo you take before throwing your phone against the wall, the figure is you. The figure's face is your face. The figure's posture is your posture. The camera was taking photos of you standing in the corner. You were the figure. You have been standing in the corner. You do not remember standing in the corner. But the photos do not lie. And the photos say you have been in every corner of this room, watching yourself, for as long as the camera has been on.

instruction: Generate a scare where a door opens to reveal something impossible.
input: Player opens a closet door expecting to find clothes.
output: The closet door opens inward. Inside: not clothes. A room. A room that should not exist — the closet is two feet deep, but the room extends thirty feet, with a ceiling that arches upward into darkness. The room has a floor of packed earth, walls of stone, and a single chair in the center. In the chair, a person. Facing away from you. The person's hair is your color, your length. The person's clothes are the clothes you wore yesterday. The person's posture is your posture — the specific way you lean forward when you are thinking. You clear your throat. The person turns. The face is your face, but wrong — the features are shifted, as if your face was photographed and then distorted, stretched, compressed. The eyes are too far apart. The mouth is too wide. The nose is crooked. It is your face, but it has been disassembled and reassembled by someone who does not understand faces. The person opens their mouth. The sound that comes out is your laugh. Not a laugh — your laugh. The specific cadence, the way you snort at the end. But the laugh goes on too long. Thirty seconds. A minute. The laugh fills the room, echoing off stone walls, getting louder, getting wronger, the pitch dropping, the cadence breaking, the laugh becoming something that is not laughter but is using the shape of laughter to express something else. The person stands. The chair falls. The person walks toward you. The closet door is still open. You are still in your hallway. The person is in the hallway now. The person is you. The closet is full of clothes. There is no room. There was never a room. But the person is still here. Standing in your hallway. Wearing your face. Laughing your laugh. And behind the person, where the closet was, there is only a wall.

instruction: Create a scare where the player realizes the face they've been talking to is not a face.
input: Player has a conversation with someone whose face is wrong.
output: You have been talking to the receptionist for three minutes. She has been answering your questions, directing you to the right office, being helpful. You thank her and turn to leave. Something makes you look back. The receptionist's face is not a face. It is a surface — smooth, featureless, the color of skin but without eyes, nose, mouth, or any opening. The surface is perfect. Undisturbed. Like a mannequin head with skin texture. But the receptionist is still talking. The voice comes from behind the surface, muffled, as if the real face is behind the mask. 'Is there anything else?' the voice asks. From the featureless surface. From a mouth that does not exist. You look around. No one else is reacting. The other receptionists, the people in the waiting room — they see a face. You see a surface. You look at the receptionist again. The surface shifts. Not muscles — the entire surface moves, reconfigures, and suddenly there is a face. A perfect face. Smiling. 'Have a good day,' the face says. The lips move. The eyes blink. The face is real. It was always real. You were wrong. You must have been wrong. But for three seconds — three seconds you cannot explain, three seconds you cannot forget — the receptionist had no face. And the voice behind the surface knew you were looking. And the voice did not care.

instruction: Design a scare where something follows the player through multiple rooms.
input: Player moves through their house and notices something is always in the room they just left.
output: You leave the kitchen. In the hallway, you glance back. Something is in the kitchen. A shape, standing where you were standing, facing away. You enter the living room. You look back into the hallway. Something is in the hallway. Same shape. Same posture. Facing away. You go upstairs. You look down. Something is at the bottom of the stairs. Same shape. Same height. Same way it holds its arms — slightly away from the body, as if it does not quite know what to do with arms. You enter the bedroom. You close the door. You wait. You open the door. Something is on the landing. It is closer now. It is on the top step. It is waiting for you to close the door again. Each time you close the door, it advances. Each time you open the door, it stops. It is playing a game. A game of advance and freeze, of closing distance while your eyes are closed. You keep the door open. The shape does not move. You stare at it. It stares at the floor — it always faces away, always shows you its back, never its face. You want to see its face. You walk toward it. One step. Two. You are on the landing. The shape is on the step below you. You reach for its shoulder. Your hand passes through. The shape is not solid. But the cold your hand feels is real — a cold so intense your fingers go numb. The shape turns. You do not see the face. You see the back of the shape's head, which is now facing you because the shape has no front. The shape is all back. No matter which angle you view it from, you see its back. It is a creature that has only a back. And it is climbing the stairs. Toward the bedroom. Toward the door you left open.

instruction: Generate a scare where the player counts objects and realizes there is one more than there should be.
input: Player counts chairs in a room they've been sitting in.
output: There are four chairs in the room. You counted them when you sat down: two against the wall, one by the desk, one by the window. Four. You have been sitting for an hour. You stand to leave and count again. Five. The fifth chair is in the corner. It was not there an hour ago. It is the same style as the others — wooden, old, slightly worn. But its seat is warm. You touch it and pull your hand back. The warmth is recent. Someone was sitting in this chair. In the corner. While you were in the room. For an hour. You did not see anyone enter. You did not hear anyone move. But the chair is warm and there are indentations in the carpet where its legs have been pressing for at least an hour. The fifth chair was here the whole time. In the corner. Behind you. Occupied. You count the chairs again. Four. The fifth chair is gone. The carpet shows no indentations. The corner is empty. But the warmth lingers in the air — you can feel it, a pocket of body heat in the corner where the chair was. Where the chair was not. Where the chair might still be, invisible, occupied, watching you count.

instruction: Create a scare where the player's pet behaves as if it can see something the player cannot.
input: Player's dog stares at an empty corner and growls.
output: The dog has been staring at the corner for twenty minutes. Not casually — intensely, the way it stares at squirrels, with every muscle tense, ears forward, lips pulled back to show teeth. The corner is empty. You have checked. You have waved your hand through the air, walked around the corner, shined a flashlight. Nothing. The dog disagrees. The dog growls — a low, continuous rumble that vibrates through its body and into the floor. The dog takes a step toward the corner. Stops. Whines. Takes another step. Its hackles are up. Its tail is between its legs. The dog is terrified of the empty corner. You sit beside the dog and look where it is looking. The corner is empty. The wall is bare. The carpet is clean. But the dog sees something. Something that is real enough to trigger the most basic instinct in an animal — the instinct that says: this is a predator, this is danger, this will hurt you. The dog barks. Once. Loud. The corner does not react. The dog barks again. The corner — the air in the corner, the light in the corner — shifts. Not visibly. Not audibly. But the shift is real because the dog stops barking and backs away, pressing against your legs, whimpering. Something in the corner has acknowledged the bark. Something in the corner has decided to move. Not toward the dog. Toward you. The dog runs. You are alone with the corner. The corner is still empty. But the air is warmer now. And there is a smell. The smell of something that has been in the corner for a very long time. The smell of old skin. The smell of patience.

instruction: Design a scare where the player opens their eyes in the dark and something is inches from their face.
input: Player wakes up in the middle of the night.
output: You open your eyes. The darkness is total. You do not know what woke you. You lie still, listening. The room is silent. Your breathing is the only sound. Then: another breathing. Not from across the room. From inches away. A slow, steady exhale that you feel on your lips, warm, moist, the breath of something that is close enough to kiss. You do not move. You do not breathe. The breathing continues — in, out, in, out, the rhythm of something patient, something that has been here for a while, something that is waiting for you to move. The darkness is too complete to see. But you can feel the displacement of air — something large is between you and the ceiling, something that is leaning over you, its face — if it has a face — inches from yours. The breathing changes. It inhales deeply. It is smelling you. The exhale is warmer, moister, the breath of something that has moved closer. Your lips tingle. The breath is touching your lips. Something is breathing into your mouth. You clamp your lips shut. The breathing stops. The presence does not leave. In the darkness, something touches your cheek. One finger. Cold. Tracing the line of your jaw. The touch is exploratory — gentle, curious, the way a blind person reads a face. The finger reaches your lips. It presses. Not hard. Just enough to say: I am here. I know you are awake. I know you can feel me. And I am not leaving until the lights come on. The lights come on. The room is empty. But on your cheek, a frost mark. In the shape of a finger.

instruction: Create a scenario where every choice the player makes leads to the same outcome.
input: Player faces a fork in the road multiple times.
output: The fork is simple: left or right. You choose left. The path leads to a clearing where a deer lies dead, its throat torn, its eyes open. You backtrack and choose right. The path leads to the same clearing. Same deer. Same wounds. You backtrack again. Left. Same clearing. You run right, past the clearing, deeper into the forest. The trees thin. You emerge into a clearing. The same clearing. The deer is still dead. You are standing at the fork again. The left path. The right path. Both lead to the dead deer. You try the center — walk straight into the forest, off the paths. The trees are thick, the undergrowth dense. You push through. The trees end. A clearing. The deer. You have been here for an hour. You have tried every direction. Every direction leads to the dead deer. You sit at the fork and wait. The deer stands. It looks at you. It is not dead — it has never been dead. It has been waiting. It walks toward you. It is not a deer anymore. Its shape is wrong — too many legs, a neck that bends the wrong way, eyes that are not on its face but on its antlers, one eye per point. The deer-thing stops in front of you. It tilts its head. 'You always choose,' it says, in a voice like branches breaking. 'You always think you choose. But there is only one clearing. There has always been only one clearing.'

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's actions are narrated by a voice that knows what they will do before they do it.
input: Player hears a narrator describing their actions in real time.
output: The voice speaks before you act. 'He reaches for the coffee cup.' You reach for the coffee cup. 'He takes a sip. It is too hot. He burns his lip.' You take a sip. It is too hot. You burn your lip. The voice is calm, clinical, the voice of a documentary narrator describing the behavior of an animal in captivity. 'He stands. He walks to the window. He looks out at the street, wondering if anyone can hear him.' You stand. You walk to the window. You look out at the street. You are wondering if anyone can hear you. The voice narrates your thoughts. 'He is afraid. He considers screaming. He decides against it. He does not know why he decides against it. He will learn: the voice has a preference for compliance.' You consider screaming. You decide against it. You do not know why. The voice continues: 'He will now try to contradict the narration. He will sit down to prove he has free will.' You refuse to sit. You stand very still. The voice waits. You stand for five minutes. Ten. The voice says nothing. You are winning. You are proving the voice wrong. You sit down. The voice resumes: 'He sits down. He was always going to sit down. The standing was a choice. The sitting was inevitable. The human body has limits. The voice has patience.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player discovers their life is on a timer they cannot see.
input: Player finds a clock that counts down to something unknown.
output: The clock is on the wall of the waiting room. You have been here for an hour. The clock counts down: 00:47:23. 00:47:22. 00:47:21. It is counting down to something. The other people in the waiting room do not look at the clock. They read magazines, check their phones, stare at the floor. The clock reaches 00:00:00. Nothing happens. The clock resets: 23:59:59. 23:59:58. Twenty-four hours. A day. The clock is counting down to something that happens once a day. You ask the receptionist about the clock. She looks at you with an expression of confusion. 'What clock?' The clock is on the wall. Right there. She looks at the wall. 'There is no clock,' she says. You point. She looks where you point. 'That is a painting,' she says. It is not a painting. It is a clock. It is counting down. 22:14:07. 22:14:06. You take a photo of the wall. The photo shows a painting. Not a clock. A painting. But your eyes see a clock. Only your eyes. Only you can see the countdown. You come back the next day. The clock is at 00:00:03. 00:00:02. 00:00:01. 00:00:00. Nothing happens. The clock resets. You come back every day for a week. Every day, the clock reaches zero. Every day, nothing happens. On day eight, something happens. Not at zero — at 00:00:03. The person sitting in the chair nearest the clock stands up, walks to the door, and leaves. The clock resets. On day nine, at 00:00:03, another person leaves. The clock is not counting down to an event. It is counting down to a person. Each day, at zero, someone leaves. And they do not come back. You check the chairs. There are fewer people in the waiting room. Each day, one fewer. The clock is counting down to you. You are the last person in the waiting room. The clock reads 23:59:59.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player tries to leave a room but keeps ending up back inside.
input: Player attempts to exit through a door.
output: You open the door and step through. The room you enter is the same room. Same furniture, same window, same clock on the wall showing 3:17 PM. You turn around. The door is behind you. You step through again. Same room. 3:17 PM. You try the window. It opens. You climb through. The other side is the room. You are inside, looking at the window you just climbed through. The clock says 3:17 PM. You try the closet. Inside the closet: the room. Smaller, but identical. You try the air vent. Inside the vent: the room, even smaller, the size of a dollhouse, but complete in every detail. The clock says 3:17 PM. You sit down. You wait. The clock does not change. 3:17 PM is forever. You try breaking the clock. The clock reforms — not repaired, reappeared, as if the room generates its own components. You try breaking the door. Another door appears. You try breaking the window. Another window. You try breaking the wall. Behind the wall: the room. The room is infinite. The room is the only room. There are no other rooms. There has never been another room. The room is the world and the world is the room and the clock says 3:17 PM and the door leads to the room and the window leads to the room and the closet leads to the room and you will be here at 3:17 PM forever.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's written words rearrange themselves.
input: Player writes in a journal and the ink moves on its own.
output: You write: 'I am safe.' The ink dries. You look away. When you look back, the words have changed: 'I AM NOT.' Same handwriting. Same pen. Different words. You cross it out and write again: 'I am in control.' The ink dries. You look away. 'YOU NEVER WERE.' You write faster: 'This is my journal, my words, my pen.' The response appears before you finish: 'THIS IS MY JOURNAL. MY WORDS. MY PEN. YOU ARE JUST THE HAND.' You throw the journal across the room. It lands open. The pages fill — not with ink, but with text, appearing as if printed, covering every page, every margin, every blank space. The text is your biography. Every moment of your life, written in your handwriting, in real time. You watch a new sentence appear: 'He threw the journal across the room because he was afraid.' Another: 'He is reading this sentence now.' Another: 'He is about to look up.' You look up. The room is empty. The journal continues: 'He expected to see something. There is nothing to see. The thing writing this is not in the room. The thing writing this is in the journal. The thing writing this is in the ink. The thing writing this is in the hand that holds the pen. The thing writing this is him. It has always been him. He is writing his own story and he does not remember writing it and he will not remember reading this.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player discovers they have been repeating the same day for years.
input: Player finds a calendar that shows every day as the same date.
output: The calendar on the wall shows today's date: March 15th. You remember March 15th from yesterday. And the day before. You check your phone: March 15th. You check your computer: March 15th. You check the newspaper: March 15th. You have been living March 15th for a long time. You do not know how long because the day resets your memory of the previous iteration. But you found the calendar — a physical calendar, hidden in the back of a drawer, where you have been writing the date every morning. The calendar shows 2,847 entries. All March 15th. You have lived this day 2,847 times. The handwriting on the calendar changes over the entries — the early ones are neat, careful, the handwriting of someone who believes the counting matters. The middle entries are messy, angry, the pen digging into the paper. The recent entries are calm, resigned. The last entry, in handwriting that matches today's, has a note: 'Day 2,847. I found the calendar again. I always find it on iteration 847. I forget on iteration 848. This is my window. Tomorrow I will forget. Today I know. The question is: what do I do with one day of knowledge in a life of forgetting?' Below the note, in a different handwriting — the same hand, but older, shakier — a response: 'You have written this note 321 times. You have never acted on it. Tomorrow you will forget. Today, try something different. Today, do not look in the mirror.' You look in the mirror. Your face is wrong — not different, wrong. The features are yours but the expression is not. The expression is the expression of someone who has lived the same day 2,847 times and has finally, finally, decided to stop.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's phone predicts actions they haven't taken yet.
input: Player's phone shows photos of things they will do today.
output: Your photo gallery has new photos. Photos of you, in places you have not been, doing things you have not done. You at the coffee shop — which you have not visited yet today. You at the park — which you have not planned to visit. You at a hospital — which you hope you will not need to visit. The photos are timestamped with today's date but future times — 2 PM, 4 PM, 7 PM. Your phone is showing you your future. You decide to contradict it. You stay home. At 2 PM, you are on your couch. Your phone vibrates. A new photo: you, on your couch, at 2 PM, looking at your phone. The photo was taken from behind you. From inside your apartment. You did not take this photo. Your phone took this photo. Without your permission. Without your knowledge. Your phone is not predicting your future. Your phone is documenting your present from a perspective you cannot access. Someone — something — is in your apartment, taking photos of you, and uploading them to your phone. The 4 PM photo shows you at the park. You refuse to go to the park. At 4 PM, you are still on your couch. The 4 PM photo updates. It now shows you on the couch. The photo adjusts. The prediction was not a prediction — it was a plan. Your phone planned for you to go to the park. When you refused, the plan changed. Your phone is not predicting your actions. Your phone is directing your actions. And when you disobey, it adjusts. The 7 PM photo shows you at the hospital. You are not going to the hospital. The photo does not update. The 7 PM photo remains: you, at the hospital, injured, on a stretcher. The photo does not adjust because the plan does not change. The hospital is inevitable. The phone knows. The phone has always known. The phone is not yours. It belongs to the thing that is planning your day.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player discovers their entire environment is a construct that deconstructs when they stop observing it.
input: Player looks away from a room and hears it changing behind them.
output: You face the wall. Behind you, the room makes a sound — not a creak, not a settle, but a rustle, the sound of paper being crumpled. You turn around. The room is the same. You face the wall again. The rustle. Louder this time. You turn. The room is the same — but the chair is slightly smaller. You face the wall. The rustle becomes a tearing sound. You turn. The chair is half its size. The bookshelf has fewer books. The window is narrower. The room is deconstructing. Not collapsing — simplifying. Losing detail. Losing substance. You spin around rapidly. The room freezes — mid-deconstruction, the chair at quarter-size, the bookshelf a skeleton, the window a slit. You spin. The room rebuilds. The chair is full-size. The books are back. But the colors are wrong — muted, desaturated, as if the room is rebuilding with less pigment. You stop spinning. The room deconstructs. Faster now. The walls lose texture. The floor loses grain. The furniture loses shape. The room is becoming a sketch — outlines, suggestions, the idea of a room without the substance. You close your eyes. The sound of deconstruction is loud now — tearing, crumbling, the sound of something being taken apart at the molecular level. You open your eyes. The room is gone. You are standing in white space. Not a white room — white space. The absence of room. The absence of anything. The room was a construct. You were the observer. Without your observation, the room had no reason to exist. You are the reason. And you stopped paying attention.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds evidence that they have been performing actions in their sleep that they cannot stop.
input: Player wakes up to find tools and materials they don't remember gathering.
output: The workbench is covered in components. Wires, circuit boards, a soldering iron (still warm), screws, a case of some kind. You do not own these things. You do not know how to solder. But the work on the bench is expert — precise, clean, the work of someone with years of experience. The device on the bench is half-built. You do not know what it is. The schematic beside it is in your handwriting. The schematic is labeled: 'DO NOT FINISH.' Below that, in the same handwriting: 'FINISH IT.' The contradictions continue on the next page: 'IT WILL KILL YOU.' 'IT WILL SAVE YOU.' 'DO NOT PLUG IT IN.' 'PLUG IT IN NOW.' You have been building something in your sleep. Something your waking mind has forbidden. Something your sleeping mind insists on. The device has a power source — a battery you do not recognize, with a label in a language you do not speak. The battery is warm. The device is almost complete. One more component. The schematic shows where it goes. The component is in your pocket. You check. It is in your pocket. A small, metallic piece, warm from your body heat. Your sleeping self put it in your pocket, knowing your waking self would find it, knowing your waking self would be unable to resist completing the device. Because the device is interesting. Because the device is beautiful. Because the device is almost done and the last piece is right here and your hands are already reaching for it. You put the piece down. Your hands pick it up. You put it down. Your hands pick it up. Your hands are not listening to you. Your hands are listening to the version of you that built this device. The version that knows what it does. The version that wrote 'FINISH IT.'
